commands.author: solodmd

target.title: fix(browser): truncate element text on code-point boundaries to avoid lone surrogates
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Three browser snapshot/text-extraction paths truncate element text content with
raw `.slice(0, N)`. When an emoji or other astral character straddles the cut
point, the raw slice keeps only the high-surrogate half, producing a lone
`\uD83D` — malformed UTF-16.

Affected paths:
- `cdp.ts`: `findCursorInteractiveElements` truncates `textContent` at 100
- `pw-tools-core.snapshot.ts`: `collectSnapshotUrls` truncates anchor label at 120
- `routes/agent.snapshot.ts`: `collectChromeMcpSnapshotUrls` truncates anchor label at 120

## Why This Change Was Made

Since the affected code runs in the browser page context (CDP `Runtime.evaluate`
/ Playwright `page.evaluate`), the SDK's `truncateUtf16Safe` is not importable.
Instead, `.replace(/[\uD800-\uDBFF]$/, "")` is appended after each `.slice()`
to strip any dangling high surrogate at the cut boundary. This preserves the
original UTF-16 code-unit cap while avoiding broken surrogate pairs.

## User Impact

No user-facing behavior change. Element text sent to the LLM now contains valid
UTF-16 regardless of where emoji fall in the truncated string. The code-unit
caps (100 / 120) remain unchanged.

## Evidence

- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm test extensions/browser` — 59 tests passed

### Real behavior proof

The proof script launches a headless Chrome instance, connects via CDP, injects
a test page with emoji at the truncation boundaries, and evaluates the **exact
same** truncation expression used in the three changed paths:

```
✅ Chrome started

─── Testing cdp.ts path (findCursorInteractiveElements) ───
Pattern: String(el.textContent || "").replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim().slice(0, 100).replace(/[\uD800-\uDBFF]$/, "")

✅ ASCII text within limit
   original: 11 units
   AFTER  fix: slice(0,100).replace() → 11 units, dangling: false ✅
✅ Long ASCII truncated
   original: 200 units
   AFTER  fix: slice(0,100).replace() → 100 units, dangling: false ✅
✅ 99x + 😀 + tail (emoji at boundary)
   original: 105 units
   ⚠️  BEFORE fix: slice(0,100) → ends with dangling surrogate (tail: 0x78 0x78 0xd83d)
   AFTER  fix: slice(0,100).replace() → 99 units, dangling: false ✅
✅ 99x + 🎉🎉 + tail (multiple emoji)
   original: 107 units
   ⚠️  BEFORE fix: slice(0,100) → ends with dangling surrogate (tail: 0x78 0x78 0xd83c)
   AFTER  fix: slice(0,100).replace() → 99 units, dangling: false ✅
✅ 51x + 25😀 + tail (code-unit cap)
   original: 105 units
   ⚠️  BEFORE fix: slice(0,100) → ends with dangling surrogate (tail: 0xd83d 0xde00 0xd83d)
   AFTER  fix: slice(0,100).replace() → 99 units, dangling: false ✅
✅ 50x + 😀 + 48y (complete pair kept)
   original: 100 units
   AFTER  fix: slice(0,100).replace() → 100 units, dangling: false ✅

📊 6/6 CDP path tests passed

─── Testing snapshot URL path (pw-tools-core.snapshot.ts:80) ───
Pattern: .slice(0, 120).replace(/[\uD800-\uDBFF]$/, "")

✅ anchor a1: before dangling=true → after dangling=false
✅ anchor a2: before dangling=true → after dangling=false
✅ anchor a3: before dangling=false → after dangling=false

📊 3/3 snapshot path tests passed

==================================================
Total: 9/9 tests passed
All outputs free of dangling surrogates: ✅
```

Each test exercises the actual OpenClaw code path expression against a real
browser page. The before-fix output shows dangling high surrogates (0xD83D,
0xD83C) that would be sent to the LLM; the after-fix output is clean.

Reproduce:
```bash
node --import tsx proof-browser-openclaw-path.mts
```

## Tests

`extensions/browser/src/browser/browser-text-truncation.test.ts` — 6 new tests
covering ASCII pass-through, long ASCII truncation, dangling-surrogate removal,
multiple emoji, code-unit cap preservation, and complete-pair pass-through.
target.author: solodmd
